ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper formalizes semi‑supervised reinforcement learning (Sec. 3) and proposes S3G (Sec. 4, Alg. 1), which infers rewards in unlabeled MDPs by treating rollouts from a policy learned in labeled MDPs as demonstrations and interleaving inverse‑RL style reward learning with policy updates. While it clearly builds on prior work (guided cost learning / IRL and MDGPS are explicitly cited and used), adapting those techniques to the semi‑supervised setting and using the agent’s own labeled‑MDP rollouts as autonomous demonstrations is a non‑trivial and well‑motivated extension. Thus the paper presents notable novelty (first practical algorithm for SSRL per the text) rather than purely incremental combination of known pieces.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is generally well organized (Intro → Related Work → Method → Experiments → Conclusion) and clearly states the main contribution (the S3G algorithm) early in the Introduction. The method section presents the core ideas with equations and a clear Algorithm 1, and the experimental setup is described concretely with task details and evaluation protocol. Presentation issues are minor but real: notation reuse is confusing (e.g. H is used both as the horizon in sums and as entropy H(π) around Equation 1), and the provided text contains repeated/duplicated sentences and formatting artifacts that hinder readability; figures and tables are referenced (Figures 2/3, Table 1) but not available in the text provided, making it hard to judge their effectiveness.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states its main claim (S3G uses inverse-RL–style reward inference from labeled-rollouts plus unlabeled experience to improve generalization) and supports it with a coherent algorithm (Eqns. 1–4, Alg.1) and empirical comparisons to sensible baselines (πRL, reward-regression, and an oracle) on multiple MuJoCo tasks. The mathematical derivations are standard and appear correct (maximum-entropy objective, exponential trajectory model in Eqn.2, and importance-sampling estimate in Eqn.3), and the policy/reward update loop is well-specified. Minor gaps remain: convergence/optimality guarantees are only inherited by appeal to prior guided-cost-learning work (no formal proof for the semi‑supervised setting where demonstrations are agent rollouts), and experimental details (e.g., statistical variation, seed counts) and full quantitative results are referenced but not shown in the provided text. Overall the technical approach is sound, with these modest missing analyses not undermining the main conclusions.
